metadata.from: Bob (Robert Hanifen) — signed 'Bob' on page 4; return address on envelope is 'Robert Hanifen, United States Fidelity and Guaranty Co., 616 Mercantile Bank Building, Dallas 1, Texas'; return address on page 4 is '3614 Noble St, Dallas, Texas'
metadata.to: John Hanifen (brother) — addressed 'Dear John' and envelope addressed to 'Cpl. John P. Hanifen 55046284'
metadata.location_sent_to: Cpl. John P. Hanifen 55046284, Co. C 439 Eng. Com. Bn., APO 301 c/o P.M., San Francisco, Calif.
metadata.envelope_notes: Air Mail envelope. Two 3-cent United States Postage stamps (Thomas Jefferson). Annotations on envelope in different handwriting: 'Walrod mustree' (or possibly 'trustee'), 'Jim', and on the side 'to hang on the [wall?]'. The envelope uses pre-printed letterhead for 'Robert Hanifen, United States Fidelity and Guaranty Co[mpany], 616 Mercantile Bank Building, Dallas 1, Texas' with some lines crossed out.
topics: Korean War — Communist guerrilla ambush near Pusan
people_mentioned: Dad (James Edward Hanifen Sr.) — fishing in Canada
Jimmy/Jim (James Edward Hanifen Jr., brother) — has a nice '51 Ford for John to drive)
Chet Miller (race car driver, fastest trial time at Indianapolis)
places_mentioned: Minnesota (Crow Wing area — fishing trip memory)
Pusan (Korea — Communist guerrilla ambush reported in newspaper)
metadata.mood_tone: Warm and brotherly, mixing lighthearted family news (fishing, car racing) with genuine concern for John's safety in Korea, frustration and anger at the draft board system, and encouraging words for John's military advancement.
metadata.legibility_notes: Most of the letter is legible with careful reading. Some words on page 1 are slightly ambiguous (e.g. 'Crow Wing' area in Minnesota). Page 2 has strong language clearly written. Page 3 has some bleed-through from the reverse side making a few words harder to read. Page 4 has bleed-through text from another page visible in the lower half but the actual letter text is clear.
metadata.historical_context: John is serving in Korea during the Korean War (1950-1953), stationed with Co. C, 439th Engineer Combat Battalion, APO 301. Bob references a newspaper report of Communist guerrillas ambushing 5 or 6 GIs outside Pusan and killing 2 or 3 of them. Bob also expresses frustration about the draft board deferring married 21-year-olds while John remains overseas. The letter is dated May 1952, during the armistice negotiations period of the Korean War. Reference to the Indianapolis 500 on Decoration Day (Memorial Day) 1952, with Chet Miller having the fastest trial time of 139.00 mph.

pages.transcription:
May 27, 1952

Dear John

Not been doing to much Lately. I wen fishing at the Local lake here in Dallas and got a 6 ounce perch. Not like the time you and me + Dad went to the Crow Wings in Minnesota. The race at Indianapolis should be real good this year. the fasest time trial was 139.00 by Chet Miller I would sure like to see them some time. I got a Card from Dad and he is in Canada trying to get a bigger fish to hang on the wall at the shop.

Say the paper say the Communist gurella ambushed about 5 or 6 G.I. just outside of Pusan and killed two or three of them. So I think you had better the gun and keep a sharp lookout for those rotten bastard. I think I know why you have to stay over there so long. These chicken shit draft board have deferred every body with a pimple on their butt. If I was you I would tell that draft board in Des Moines to kiss your ass. Every time I see these guys

in the reserve or National, on there 21 years old that are married and deferred I get pissed off. Jimmy says he has a nice 51 Ford for you to drive when you get home. You two are lucky for that. This old 46 Chrysler I got isn't worth a good shit. I hope you and Jim come down to see me when you get home. I will be home Christmas I hope. So you keep up the good work and try and beat the old man rank of Sergeant. Well John good by for now and I will send you the result of the big race
over

on Decoration day at Indianapolis

Love Your Brother

Bob

P.S. Keep your eyes open for those sneaking red bastards.

3614 Noble St
Dallas, Texas

full_transcription:
May 27, 1952

Dear John

Not been doing to much Lately. I wen fishing at the Local lake here in Dallas and got a 6 ounce perch. Not like the time you and me + Dad went to the Crow Wings in Minnesota. The race at Indianapolis should be real good this year. the fasest time trial was 139.00 by Chet Miller I would sure like to see them some time. I got a Card from Dad and he is in Canada trying to get a bigger fish to hang on the wall at the shop.

Say the paper say the Communist gurella ambushed about 5 or 6 G.I. just outside of Pusan and killed two or three of them. So I think you had better the gun and keep a sharp lookout for those rotten bastard. I think I know why you have to stay over there so long. These chicken shit draft board have deferred every body with a pimple on their butt. If I was you I would tell that draft board in Des Moines to kiss your ass. Every time I see these guys

in the reserve or National, on there 21 years old that are married and deferred I get pissed off. Jimmy says he has a nice 51 Ford for you to drive when you get home. You two are lucky for that. This old 46 Chrysler I got isn't worth a good shit. I hope you and Jim come down to see me when you get home. I will be home Christmas I hope. So you keep up the good work and try and beat the old man rank of Sergeant. Well John good by for now and I will send you the result of the big race

on Decoration day at Indianapolis

Love Your Brother

Bob

P.S. Keep your eyes open for those sneaking red bastards.

3614 Noble St
Dallas, Texas
summary: This is a May 1952 letter from Bob (Robert Hanifen) in Dallas, Texas to his brother John, an Army corporal serving in Korea with the 439th Engineer Combat Battalion. Bob shares lighthearted family news — a meager fishing catch, their father's trip to Canada to fish, and excitement about the upcoming Indianapolis 500 — before turning serious about a newspaper report of Communist guerrillas ambushing GIs near Pusan and urging John to stay alert. Bob vents anger at the draft board system for deferring stateside civilians while John remains overseas, and closes with encouragement for John to achieve the rank of Sergeant, promising to send the race results from Indianapolis on Decoration Day.
